past_medical_history: Iron-deficiency anemia treated with oral supplementation at age 19
Migraine without aura, infrequent episodes (1-2 per year)
Hypothyroidism diagnosed at age 20, well-controlled on levothyroxine
family_history: Mother: Hashimoto thyroiditis, type 2 diabetes mellitus
No family history of epilepsy, psychosis, or neurodegenerative disease

social_history.alcohol: Occasional social drinking, 1-2 drinks per month
social_history.living_situation: Lives with roommate in university housing
social_history.exercise: Regular yoga and walking, 3-4 times per week

neurological_exam.mental_status: GCS 12/15 (E3 V4 M5). Alert but disoriented to time, place, and situation. Fluctuating attention — intermittently follows simple commands, then becomes unresponsive to verbal stimuli. Speech is disorganized with paraphasic errors and perseveration. Attempts at conversation reveal paranoid ideation and visual hallucinations (reports seeing insects on the walls). Unable to perform serial 7s or spell WORLD backwards. Short-term memory severely impaired; cannot recall any of 3 objects at 1 minute.
neurological_exam.cranial_nerves: Pupils 4 mm bilaterally, reactive to light. Extraocular movements intact with occasional slow saccades. No nystagmus. Facial sensation intact to light touch bilaterally. Intermittent involuntary orolingual-facial dyskinesias observed — lip smacking, jaw clenching, and tongue protrusions occurring every 10-15 seconds. Hearing grossly intact. Palate elevates symmetrically. Sternocleidomastoid and trapezius strength 5/5 bilaterally. Tongue midline without fasciculations.
neurological_exam.motor: Tone mildly increased in bilateral upper extremities with intermittent rigidity. Strength 4+/5 throughout bilateral upper and lower extremities, limited by cooperation. Involuntary choreoathetoid movements of bilateral hands and intermittent dystonic posturing of the left wrist noted. No asterixis.
neurological_exam.sensory: Withdraws to pain in all four extremities. Light touch and pinprick testing unreliable due to patient agitation and fluctuating cooperation.
neurological_exam.reflexes: Deep tendon reflexes 2+ and symmetric throughout. Plantar responses flexor bilaterally. No clonus.
neurological_exam.coordination: Unable to assess finger-to-nose or heel-to-shin reliably due to involuntary movements and poor cooperation. No obvious cerebellar dysmetria observed on gross testing.
neurological_exam.gait: Unable to assess; patient is not ambulatory due to encephalopathy and agitation. Requires physical restraint at times to prevent self-injury.
neurological_exam.additional: Autonomic observations: Heart rate fluctuates between 65 and 115 bpm during examination without clear precipitant. Blood pressure varies from 105/68 to 142/90 mmHg over 30-minute observation period. No diaphoresis or significant temperature fluctuation noted during exam. Intermittent central hypoventilation episodes with respiratory rate dropping to 10 breaths per minute, followed by periods of tachypnea at 22-24 breaths per minute.

patient.chief_complaint: Convulsions, agitation, and rapidly progressive confusion over three weeks
patient.history_present_illness: A 23-year-old woman with a history of well-controlled hypothyroidism and infrequent migraines is brought to the emergency department by her roommate and parents for worsening behavioral changes, confusion, and new-onset seizures. Approximately three weeks prior to presentation, the patient developed a mild upper respiratory tract illness with dry cough, rhinorrhea, and low-grade malaise lasting about five days, which appeared to resolve spontaneously without medical attention. About two weeks before presentation, her roommate noticed the patient becoming increasingly irritable, withdrawn, and sleeping excessively during the day while experiencing severe insomnia at night. The patient began missing classes and complained of difficulty concentrating. Her parents, who live in a different city, were contacted when the roommate found the patient talking to herself and expressing paranoid beliefs that her food was being poisoned. She was evaluated at a campus health center approximately ten days prior, where she was diagnosed with an acute stress reaction and prescribed low-dose lorazepam for sleep, which provided minimal benefit. Five days before the current presentation, the patient's confusion markedly worsened. She was unable to recognize her roommate, became increasingly agitated, and exhibited bizarre repetitive movements of her mouth and hands. Two days before presentation, the patient was witnessed to have a generalized tonic-clonic seizure lasting approximately two minutes with spontaneous termination. She was taken to a local emergency department where she received intravenous lorazepam and a loading dose of levetiracetam. A non-contrast CT head was reported as unremarkable. She was discharged home with oral levetiracetam and instructions for outpatient neurology follow-up. However, she had a second witnessed generalized tonic-clonic seizure earlier today and was found by her parents to be markedly confused, combative, and exhibiting continuous involuntary facial and limb movements. She was brought to this tertiary emergency department by ambulance. Of note, risperidone 1 mg twice daily was started by the outside emergency physician for agitation and suspected psychosis during the prior visit, without notable improvement in her psychiatric symptoms. No recent travel, tick bites, or animal exposures. No history of substance use. No prior psychiatric hospitalizations or similar episodes.

background.posterior_dominant_rhythm: Poorly organized, 5-6 Hz theta range activity replacing normal alpha rhythm
background.symmetry: Broadly symmetric with mild left hemispheric predominance of slowing
background.reactivity: Minimal reactivity to auditory and tactile stimulation

findings.morphology: Semi-rhythmic, moderate to high amplitude (100-200 mcV)
findings.clinical_correlation: Indicates diffuse cerebral dysfunction consistent with encephalopathy

findings.location: Left temporal onset with rapid bilateral spread
findings.morphology: Rhythmic sharp waves evolving in frequency and amplitude over 30-60 second epochs
findings.state: Two subclinical electrographic seizures captured during 60-minute recording
findings.clinical_correlation: Subclinical seizure activity requiring treatment adjustment

artifacts.note: Intermittent due to involuntary orofacial movements, partially obscuring underlying activity

activating_procedures.hyperventilation: Not performed due to patient's impaired level of consciousness and poor cooperation

eeg.impression: Abnormal EEG demonstrating moderate-to-severe diffuse encephalopathy with generalized rhythmic delta activity and two subclinical electrographic seizures of left temporal onset. Background is diffusely slow and disorganized. No extreme delta brush pattern identified, though muscle artifact limits interpretation in some epochs. The combination of GRDA and subclinical seizures is non-specific but may be seen in metabolic encephalopathy, infectious encephalitis, or autoimmune encephalitis.
eeg.limitations: Intermittent muscle artifact from orofacial dyskinesias partially obscures frontal and temporal channels. Recording duration limited to 60 minutes; continuous EEG monitoring recommended.
recommended_actions: Continuous EEG monitoring for 24-48 hours to assess seizure burden
Consider antiseizure medication optimization given subclinical seizure activity
Clinical correlation with CSF analysis and MRI to narrow differential

findings.location: Bilateral periventricular white matter, predominantly surrounding the frontal and occipital horns of the lateral ventricles
findings.size: Multifocal areas ranging from 5 to 15 mm

findings.location: Bilateral basal ganglia, predominantly caudate heads and putamina
Right mesial temporal lobe, hippocampal region

signal_characteristics.FLAIR: Equivocal — may represent artifact versus true signal change

additional_observations: No acute infarction on DWI sequences
No evidence of hemorrhage on SWI
Ventricles normal in size and configuration
Mild mucosal thickening in bilateral maxillary sinuses, likely incidental
Mild vasogenic edema surrounding periventricular lesions
mri.impression: Multifocal bilateral T2/FLAIR signal abnormalities involving periventricular white matter and bilateral basal ganglia with associated vasogenic edema, without enhancement or restricted diffusion. Equivocal right mesial temporal signal change. The differential includes autoimmune or inflammatory encephalitis, demyelinating disease (acute disseminated encephalomyelitis), CNS vasculitis, or less likely viral encephalitis. The periventricular and basal ganglia distribution is somewhat atypical for classic HSV encephalitis. Clinical and serological correlation recommended.

differential_by_imaging.reasoning: Multifocal periventricular and basal ganglia involvement can be seen in anti-NMDAR and other autoimmune encephalitides
Multifocal white matter lesions following a viral prodrome; however, basal ganglia involvement is less typical
Multifocal lesions could represent small vessel vasculitis, though absence of infarction argues against
Distribution atypical for HSV; absent temporal lobe predominance and lack of hemorrhagic changes

recommended_actions: CSF analysis including viral PCR panel and autoimmune encephalitis antibody panel
MR angiography if vasculitis remains a concern
Follow-up MRI in 4-6 weeks to assess evolution of lesions
Consider CT chest/abdomen/pelvis for paraneoplastic workup if autoimmune encephalitis confirmed

findings: Sinus tachycardia at upper end of normal range
ecg.interpretation: Normal sinus rhythm at 96 bpm with borderline QTc prolongation. No acute ischemic changes.
ecg.clinical_correlation: Borderline QTc prolongation may be secondary to risperidone use. Monitor if additional QTc-prolonging agents are initiated. Sinus tachycardia may reflect agitation, autonomic instability, or medication effect.

CBC.clinical_significance: Mild leukocytosis, may indicate physiological stress or infection

BMP.clinical_significance: Mild hyponatremia, may suggest SIADH in setting of CNS pathology

Thyroid.clinical_significance: Mildly elevated TSH, may indicate suboptimal levothyroxine dosing or intercurrent illness; unlikely to explain encephalopathy at this level
Low-normal free T4 with elevated TSH suggests mild subclinical hypothyroidism

Inflammatory Markers.clinical_significance: Mildly elevated, non-specific — may reflect systemic inflammation or recent viral illness

Other.clinical_significance: Mildly elevated, likely secondary to seizure activity or agitation; does not support neuroleptic malignant syndrome (would expect markedly elevated >1000)
Elevated, may be secondary to risperidone use or post-ictal; interpret with caution

labs.interpretation: Mild leukocytosis with neutrophilia likely reflecting physiological stress. Mild hyponatremia (131 mEq/L) raises concern for SIADH in the setting of CNS pathology. Mildly elevated TSH with low-normal free T4 likely reflects known hypothyroidism with possible under-replacement, though insufficient to explain encephalopathy. CRP mildly elevated. Mildly elevated CK consistent with recent seizure activity. Elevated prolactin likely secondary to risperidone. Toxicology screen negative. Hepatic and renal function normal.

special_tests.Cytology: No malignant cells; reactive lymphocytes noted
special_tests.Oligoclonal_bands: Present (3 unique CSF bands not in serum)

csf.interpretation: Mild lymphocytic pleocytosis with borderline elevated protein, normal glucose, and normal opening pressure. Oligoclonal bands present with mildly elevated IgG index, suggesting intrathecal immunoglobulin synthesis. Infectious etiologies (HSV, VZV, enterovirus, bacterial, fungal, syphilis) are negative on initial testing. These findings are consistent with an inflammatory or autoimmune process affecting the CNS. Anti-NMDA receptor antibody testing has been sent to a reference laboratory and results are pending.

Anti-NMDAR Antibody Panel.clinical_significance: Positive serum anti-NMDA receptor antibodies strongly support the diagnosis of anti-NMDA receptor encephalitis
Positive CSF anti-NMDA receptor antibodies are the gold standard for diagnosis; CSF positivity is more specific than serum

output.interpretation: Both serum and CSF anti-NMDA receptor IgG antibodies are positive, confirming the diagnosis of anti-NMDA receptor encephalitis. CSF testing is considered more specific and sensitive than serum alone. These results, in conjunction with the clinical presentation (subacute onset of psychiatric symptoms, seizures, movement disorder, and autonomic instability in a young woman), are diagnostic.

Pelvic Ultrasound.value: Normal size (3.2 x 2.1 x 1.8 cm), multiple small follicles, no dominant mass
Mildly enlarged (4.1 x 2.8 x 2.5 cm) with a 2.3 cm complex cystic-solid lesion, mixed echogenicity with internal debris and possible calcification
Pelvic Ultrasound.clinical_significance: Complex ovarian lesion concerning for mature cystic teratoma (dermoid cyst); surgical evaluation recommended given association with anti-NMDAR encephalitis

Pelvic Ultrasound.value: Normal size and appearance, endometrial thickness 8 mm
Small amount of physiologic free fluid in cul-de-sac

output.interpretation: A 2.3 cm complex cystic-solid lesion is identified in the left ovary, with imaging features most consistent with a mature cystic teratoma (dermoid cyst). In the context of confirmed anti-NMDA receptor encephalitis, ovarian teratoma is found in approximately 40-50% of affected young women and is considered a trigger for the autoimmune response. Gynecologic surgical consultation for oophorectomy is recommended, as tumor removal is associated with improved neurological outcomes and reduced relapse rates.
abnormal_values_summary: Left ovary: 2.3 cm complex cystic-solid lesion, likely mature cystic teratoma

Autoimmune Encephalitis Panel.clinical_significance: Mildly elevated, consistent with known Hashimoto thyroiditis; does not indicate Hashimoto encephalopathy in isolation
Low-titer ANA, non-specific; may be seen in autoimmune-prone individuals; does not indicate systemic lupus in isolation

output.interpretation: Comprehensive autoimmune encephalitis antibody panel confirms anti-NMDA receptor antibody positivity and is negative for other neuronal surface antibody syndromes (LGI1, CASPR2, GABA-B, AMPA, DPPX). Mildly elevated anti-TPO antibodies are consistent with the patient's known Hashimoto thyroiditis but do not support a diagnosis of Hashimoto encephalopathy given the confirmed anti-NMDAR antibody positivity and clinical syndrome. Low-titer ANA is non-specific and likely incidental in this autoimmune-prone individual.

signal_characteristics.density: Mixed fat and soft tissue density with internal calcification (Hounsfield units -40 to +60)

additional_observations: Lungs are clear without nodules or infiltrates
No pathological retroperitoneal or pelvic lymphadenopathy
No free fluid in the abdomen
output.impression: Well-circumscribed mixed-density lesion in the left adnexa measuring 2.4 x 2.0 cm, containing fat, soft tissue, and calcification, most consistent with a mature cystic teratoma (dermoid cyst). No other suspicious masses, lymphadenopathy, or paraneoplastic findings elsewhere in the chest, abdomen, or pelvis.

differential_by_imaging.reasoning: Fat-containing adnexal lesion with calcification is characteristic
Fat component and calcification pattern strongly favor teratoma

recommended_actions: Gynecologic oncology consultation for surgical planning
Tumor removal is associated with improved outcomes in anti-NMDAR encephalitis

special_tests.Oligoclonal_bands: Present (5 unique CSF bands, increased from 3 on initial LP)

output.interpretation: Repeat lumbar puncture shows progressive lymphocytic pleocytosis and rising protein, with increasing oligoclonal bands and IgG index, confirming ongoing intrathecal immune activation. Anti-NMDA receptor IgG antibodies are positive in the CSF. These findings are consistent with active anti-NMDA receptor encephalitis. The progressive CSF changes suggest the disease is still in an active inflammatory phase and support aggressive immunotherapy.

output.query: anti-NMDA receptor encephalitis treatment guidelines first-line second-line immunotherapy

results.title: A clinical approach to diagnosis of autoimmune encephalitis
results.authors: Graus F, Titulaer MJ, Balu R, et al.
results.key_findings: Proposed diagnostic criteria for autoimmune encephalitis. First-line treatment: IV methylprednisolone, IVIG, or plasma exchange. Second-line: rituximab or cyclophosphamide if poor response to first-line within 2 weeks.

results.title: Treatment and prognostic factors for long-term outcome in patients with anti-NMDA receptor encephalitis
results.authors: Titulaer MJ, McCracken L, Gabilondo I, et al.
results.key_findings: In 577 patients, 53% improved with first-line immunotherapy. Tumor removal in teratoma-associated cases improved outcomes. Second-line immunotherapy beneficial when first-line fails. 81% had good outcome at 24 months. Early treatment associated with better outcomes.

results.title: Anti-NMDA receptor encephalitis: diagnosis, management, and long-term outcomes
results.authors: Dalmau J, Armangue T, Planaguma J, et al.
results.key_findings: ICU admission required in ~75% of cases. Ovarian teratoma present in ~40% of females >12 years. Relapses occur in ~12% of cases. First-line: corticosteroids + IVIG or plasma exchange. Second-line: rituximab preferred over cyclophosphamide.

output.summary: Anti-NMDA receptor encephalitis treatment follows a stepwise immunotherapy approach. First-line therapy consists of high-dose IV methylprednisolone (1g/day for 5 days), intravenous immunoglobulin (0.4 g/kg/day for 5 days), or plasma exchange (5-7 sessions). If no improvement within 2 weeks, second-line therapy with rituximab (375 mg/m2 weekly for 4 weeks) or cyclophosphamide (750 mg/m2 monthly) is recommended. Tumor removal (if present) is critical and associated with faster recovery and fewer relapses. ICU-level care is often required for autonomic instability, seizure management, and airway protection. Benzodiazepines are preferred over antipsychotics for agitation, as antipsychotics may worsen dyskinesias. Long-term maintenance immunosuppression (mycophenolate or azathioprine) should be considered to prevent relapse.

output.proposed: Risperidone 1 mg twice daily for agitation
interactions: Risperidone + Levothyroxine: Minor interaction — risperidone may slightly decrease thyroid hormone levels; monitor TSH
Risperidone + Levetiracetam: No clinically significant pharmacokinetic interaction; both can lower seizure threshold awareness
contraindications: Caution in anti-NMDA receptor encephalitis: antipsychotics may worsen movement disorders (dyskinesias, dystonia) and can trigger or mimic neuroleptic malignant syndrome in this population
Risperidone contributes to QTc prolongation (current QTc 448 ms); avoid combining with other QTc-prolonging agents
warnings: Antipsychotics are generally NOT recommended as first-line for agitation in suspected autoimmune encephalitis — benzodiazepines preferred
May mask or exacerbate neuropsychiatric symptoms, complicating clinical assessment
Risk of dystonic reactions and NMS-like presentations, which can confuse the clinical picture
output.formulary_status: On formulary; however, use in autoimmune encephalitis should be avoided per expert consensus
alternatives: Lorazepam 1-2 mg IV/IM q4-6h PRN for agitation (preferred in autoimmune encephalitis)
Midazolam 2-5 mg IV/IM for acute severe agitation
Dexmedetomidine infusion for ICU-level sedation if needed

output.query: differentiating anti-NMDA receptor encephalitis from viral encephalitis clinical features MRI EEG

results.title: Distinguishing anti-NMDAR encephalitis from viral encephalitis
results.authors: Gable MS, Sheriff H, Dalmau J, et al.
results.key_findings: Of patients initially diagnosed with viral encephalitis, 1% had anti-NMDAR encephalitis. Key distinguishing features: movement disorders, psychiatric symptoms preceding neurologic decline, younger female patients, and EEG showing extreme delta brush in NMDAR vs temporal periodic discharges in HSV.

output.summary: Anti-NMDA receptor encephalitis and viral encephalitis (particularly HSV) can present similarly with encephalopathy, seizures, and CSF pleocytosis. Key differentiators: (1) Anti-NMDAR tends to have psychiatric prodrome followed by movement disorder and autonomic instability, whereas HSV presents with acute fever, headache, and rapid deterioration; (2) MRI in HSV shows unilateral or bilateral medial temporal lobe and orbitofrontal changes with hemorrhage, whereas NMDAR MRI may be normal or show non-specific multifocal changes; (3) CSF in HSV shows higher protein and may have RBCs, while NMDAR shows milder pleocytosis with intrathecal antibody synthesis; (4) EEG in HSV shows periodic lateralized epileptiform discharges (PLEDs) from temporal regions, while NMDAR may show extreme delta brush pattern.

differential.key_distinguishing: CSF HSV PCR negative, MRI lacks temporal lobe hemorrhagic changes, clinical trajectory with psychiatric prodrome and orofacial dyskinesias is more typical of autoimmune encephalitis

differential.diagnosis: Primary psychiatric disorder (first-break psychosis or schizophrenia)
differential.key_distinguishing: Presence of seizures, movement disorder, fluctuating consciousness, autonomic instability, and CSF pleocytosis are not features of primary psychiatric disease; poor response to antipsychotics further argues against
Absence of high fever, marked rigidity, and severely elevated CK (>1000 U/L); CK only mildly elevated at 185 U/L; symptoms preceded risperidone initiation

LGI1 typically presents with faciobrachial dystonic seizures and hyponatremia in older adults; CASPR2 associated with peripheral nerve hyperexcitability; MRI pattern of periventricular and basal ganglia involvement differs from medial temporal lobe predilection of LGI1

differential.diagnosis: Hashimoto encephalopathy (steroid-responsive encephalopathy associated with autoimmune thyroiditis)
differential.key_distinguishing: Patient has known Hashimoto thyroiditis with mildly elevated anti-TPO, but the presence of positive anti-NMDAR antibodies, orofacial dyskinesias, and specific MRI pattern favor anti-NMDAR encephalitis; Hashimoto encephalopathy is a diagnosis of exclusion

ADEM typically presents with multifocal neurologic deficits and large demyelinating lesions post-infection; does not typically produce psychiatric symptoms, orofacial dyskinesias, or autonomic instability as prominent features

optimal_actions.action: Stabilize patient: secure airway, establish IV access, continuous monitoring. Administer IV lorazepam for seizure control and agitation; avoid antipsychotics.
Order EEG to evaluate for subclinical seizures and encephalopathic patterns
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Generalized slowing, GRDA, possible subclinical seizures; no extreme delta brush (moderate difficulty)

optimal_actions.action: Order brain MRI with contrast to evaluate structural and inflammatory changes
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Multifocal T2/FLAIR signal changes in periventricular white matter and basal ganglia; atypical for HSV

optimal_actions.action: Perform lumbar puncture with CSF analysis including cell count, protein, glucose, viral PCR panel, and send for anti-NMDAR antibodies
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Mild lymphocytic pleocytosis, borderline elevated protein, negative viral PCR, oligoclonal bands present

optimal_actions.action: Order comprehensive laboratory workup including CBC, BMP, thyroid function, inflammatory markers, CK, toxicology screen
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Mild hyponatremia suggesting SIADH, mild leukocytosis, mildly elevated TSH (confounding), negative toxicology

optimal_actions.action: Request anti-NMDA receptor antibody testing in serum and CSF
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Positive anti-NMDAR IgG in both serum and CSF, confirming diagnosis

optimal_actions.action: Order pelvic imaging (ultrasound or CT) to screen for ovarian teratoma
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Left ovarian complex cystic-solid lesion consistent with mature cystic teratoma

optimal_actions.action: Initiate first-line immunotherapy: IV methylprednisolone 1g/day for 5 days and IVIG 0.4 g/kg/day for 5 days
Discontinue risperidone and switch to benzodiazepines for agitation management
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Antipsychotics contraindicated in anti-NMDAR encephalitis; may worsen dyskinesias

optimal_actions.action: Consult gynecologic oncology for surgical removal of ovarian teratoma
Arrange ICU admission for continuous monitoring of autonomic instability and airway protection
Search medical literature for NMDAR encephalitis treatment protocols and prognostic factors
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Stepwise immunotherapy approach, importance of tumor removal, ICU care guidelines

critical_actions: Perform CSF analysis and send anti-NMDAR antibodies
Initiate first-line immunotherapy (IV corticosteroids + IVIG or plasma exchange) without waiting for antibody results if clinical suspicion is high
Screen for and remove ovarian teratoma if present
Discontinue antipsychotics (risperidone) and use benzodiazepines for agitation
Arrange continuous EEG monitoring to detect subclinical seizures
ICU admission for autonomic monitoring and airway protection
contraindicated_actions: Continuing or escalating antipsychotic medications (risperidone, haloperidol) — may worsen dyskinesias and mimic NMS
Discharging the patient for outpatient follow-up without initiating immunotherapy and workup
Attributing the presentation solely to a primary psychiatric disorder without ruling out autoimmune encephalitis
Administering carbamazepine or phenytoin for seizure control without checking drug interactions (QTc prolongation risk, hepatic enzyme induction may affect immunotherapy)
Delaying immunotherapy while waiting for antibody results in a clinically compatible case
key_reasoning_points: The triad of psychiatric symptoms, new-onset seizures, and orofacial dyskinesias in a young woman should prompt immediate consideration of anti-NMDA receptor encephalitis
A preceding viral prodrome is common in anti-NMDAR encephalitis and does not exclude an autoimmune etiology
The poor response to risperidone is a clinical clue — primary psychosis would be expected to show at least partial response to antipsychotics
CSF lymphocytic pleocytosis with oligoclonal bands points to intrathecal immune activation rather than a primary psychiatric or metabolic etiology
The MRI pattern of periventricular and basal ganglia involvement differs from the medial temporal lobe predilection seen in HSV encephalitis and LGI1 antibody encephalitis

Mildly elevated TSH with known Hashimoto thyroiditis is a confounding factor but insufficient to explain the clinical syndrome; however, autoimmune thyroiditis increases the pre-test probability of other autoimmune conditions
Mild hyponatremia (SIADH) is commonly seen in anti-NMDAR encephalitis and other CNS inflammatory conditions
Antipsychotics should be avoided in suspected anti-NMDAR encephalitis as they can exacerbate movement disorders and trigger NMS-like presentations
Ovarian teratoma screening is mandatory in all females with anti-NMDAR encephalitis, as tumor removal improves outcomes and reduces relapse
The mildly elevated CK (185 U/L) is consistent with seizure activity but far below the levels expected in neuroleptic malignant syndrome (typically >1000 U/L)

metadata.difficulty_description: Moderate difficulty: MRI shows multifocal periventricular and basal ganglia changes rather than the classic temporal pattern, creating overlap with ADEM and vasculitis. TSH is mildly elevated due to known hypothyroidism, creating a confounding finding (Hashimoto encephalopathy as a differential). Anti-NMDAR antibodies are pending on initial CSF, requiring the agent to pursue follow-up testing. No extreme delta brush on EEG. The prior treatment with risperidone introduces diagnostic confusion with neuroleptic malignant syndrome.
